messages.content:
What supplies the DP Flap?
a branch of the internal mammary artery
a branch of the thyrocervical artery
a branch of the thoracoacromial artery
a branch of the transverse cervical artery
A branch of the thoracoacromial artery supplies the DP flap.
is it not the first  choice?

The choice of artery for supplying the DP flap may vary depending on the particular case and surgeon's preference. However, the thoracoacromial artery is often preferred because it is a reliable source of blood supply and the flap is easier to harvest. In some cases, other arteries such as the internal mammary or transverse cervical artery may be used if the thoracoacromial artery is not suitable or cannot be spared. Ultimately, the decision about which artery to use is made based on the individual patient's anatomy and the surgical technique used.

True of Full Thickness Skin graft used in the face.
commonly taken from the supraclavicular & postauricular area
best taken from the thighs, upper arm, buttocks, scalp & abdomen
taken approximately on the superficial dermis (approx. 0.012 in)
hair follicle bulb is usually not included

The first statement is incorrect. Full thickness skin grafts are not commonly taken from the supraclavicular and postauricular areas for use in the face. These areas have thicker skin which may not provide results that blend well with the surrounding facial skin. 

The second statement is more accurate. Full thickness skin grafts for use in the face are commonly taken from the thighs, upper arm, buttocks, scalp, and abdomen. These areas provide a good match for the skin on the face, especially in terms of thickness and texture.

The third statement is generally correct. Full thickness skin grafts are taken from the superficial dermis, usually at a depth of approximately 0.012 inches (0.3 mm). 

The fourth statement is also generally correct. When using full thickness skin grafts, the hair follicle bulb is usually not included to avoid hair growth in the transplanted area which can cause noticeable differences in texture and color when compared to the surrounding facial skin.

True of STSG.
best to use in a soft tissue deprived surgical defect
taken approximately on the superficial dermis
commonly taken from the supraclavicular & postauricular area
hair follicle bulb is usually included

The first statement is generally correct. Split-thickness skin grafts (STSG) are commonly used in soft tissue deprived surgical defects because they are more pliable and elastic than full thickness skin grafts.

The second statement is generally correct. STSGs are taken from the superficial dermis and a portion of the underlying subcutaneous fat, usually at a depth of approximately 0.008 inches (0.2 mm).

The third statement is incorrect. STSGs are not commonly taken from the supraclavicular and postauricular areas because these areas have thicker skin which may not provide the desired pliability or color match for the recipient site.

The fourth statement is incorrect. STSGs do not include hair follicle bulbs because they are taken from the superficial layer of skin where hair follicles are not present.

An atopic 25 years old filipino female with a low nasal bridge came to your clinic and wanted to have an Augmentation Rhinoplasty. 
What is the best implant to use in this case?
goretex
costal cartilage
septal cartilage
silicone
aural cartilage

The best implant to use for augmentation rhinoplasty in this case would depend on several factors, including the patient's preferences, medical history, and nasal anatomy. However, given that the patient is atopic and has a low nasal bridge, it may be preferable to use a non-biological implant material to minimize the risk of infection or rejection. 

In this scenario, Gore-Tex and silicone are commonly used implant materials for nasal bridge augmentation due to their biocompatibility, low risk of reaction, and ability to maintain shape over time. However, the choice may also depend on the surgeon's experience and preference.

Costal cartilage, septal cartilage, and aural cartilage are biological materials that can be used for nasal bridge augmentation, but may be associated with higher risk of complications such as warping, resorption, or infections, especially in atopic patients. Therefore, the use of biological materials may be reserved for cases where synthetic materials are not preferred or feasible.

A 34 year old filipino male came to your clinic with a complaint of nasal deformity which resulted from a mauling incident which happened 2 years prior. On examination, the septum was severely deviated and the nasal bridge was depressed. What is the best procedure to do in this case?
Reduction Rhinoplasty
Septoplasty
Augmentataion Rhinoplasty
Septorhinoplasty

Based on the nasal examination findings, the best procedure to do in this case would be a Septorhinoplasty. This procedure involves both the correction of the nasal septum deviation through Septoplasty, and the correction of the external deformities through Rhinoplasty. 

Since the patient has both a deviated nasal septum and a depressed nasal bridge, a combination of correction procedures is necessary. A Septoplasty, which involves straightening and repositioning the nasal septum, will address the deviated septum, while a Rhinoplasty will address the external deformities such as the depressed nasal bridge. 

As the patient's nasal deformity resulted from a traumatic incident, it is important to consider the functional aspects of the nose in addition to the aesthetic goals. Therefore, addressing both the septal deviation and external deformities would ensure restoration of both form and function of the nose.

A caucasian 29 year old female came to your clinic and wanted to have her nose hump treated. What would be the best procedure would you suggest?
Reduction Rhinoplasty
Septoplasty
Augmentation Rhinoplasty
Septorhinoplasty

Based on the patient's concern of a nasal hump, the best procedure to suggest in this case would be a Reduction Rhinoplasty. Reduction Rhinoplasty is a type of nose surgery that involves removing any excess bone, cartilage, or tissue from the nasal hump. 

Reduction Rhinoplasty is specifically recommended for patients who wish to correct a nasal hump without altering other features of their nose. This type of surgical procedure is performed under local anesthesia. 

It is important to conduct a comprehensive nasal examination and review a patient's medical history to ensure that Reduction Rhinoplasty is the most appropriate procedure for the patient. Additionally, to address any underlying functional issues that the patient may have, a Septorhinoplasty may be recommended if the examination finds that there are any functional issues, such as breathing problems, that need to be addressed.

True of Cleft Lip and Palate.
does not occur in combination
is always related to other congenital anomaly
only happens to children of low income families
is a non fusion of the frontonasal and maxillary prominence

The fourth statement is generally correct. Cleft lip and palate is a congenital anomaly characterized by a non-fusion of the frontonasal and maxillary prominence in utero, resulting in a separation or clefting of the upper lip and/or palate. 

The first statement is incorrect. Cleft lip and palate can occur in combination or separately. 

The second statement is partially correct. While cleft lip and/or palate may be associated with other congenital anomalies, this is not always the case. 

The third statement is incorrect. Cleft lip and palate can occur in any socioeconomic group and are not exclusively limited to low-income families.

Maxillary prominence arises from which branchial arch?
second branchial arch
third branchial arch
first branchial arch
fourth branchial arch

The maxillary prominence arises from the first branchial arch during embryonic development. The first branchial arch also forms other structures such as the mandible, muscles of mastication, and parts of the ear.

True of Nasoalveolar molding (NAM)
a surgical method of reshaping the gums, lips and nostrils for cleft lip & palate patients
intended to reduce severity of the oronasal deformity in cleft lip & palate patients
is best applied six months after birth
none of the above

The second statement is correct. Nasoalveolar molding (NAM) is a non-surgical orthodontic technique used to reduce the severity of the oronasal deformity in infants with cleft lip and palate. NAM involves using specialized orthodontic appliances and gentle pressure to mold the gums, lips, and nostrils into their proper position before surgery. 

The first statement is incorrect. NAM is a non-surgical technique that does not involve reshaping the gums, lips, or nostrils through surgery.

The third statement is incorrect. The best results with NAM are obtained when the technique is started within 1-2 weeks after birth, and continued for several months until surgery is performed. 

Therefore, the correct statement is the second one: Nasoalveolar molding (NAM) is intended to reduce the severity of the oronasal deformity in cleft lip and palate patients.

If needed, what is the ideal time to do rhinoplasty in cleft lip & palate patients?
three to seven years old
seven to ten years old
eleven to fifteen years old
eighteen years old and older

The ideal time to perform rhinoplasty in cleft lip and palate patients generally depends on the specific case and the patient's age. However, the third option, eleven to fifteen years old, is generally considered an appropriate age range for rhinoplasty in cleft lip and palate patients, as this is a time when the face is more fully developed and growth is typically stable.

In general, it is important to wait until the child's facial and nasal growth has progressed enough to ensure that the final result will be stable and long-lasting. This is usually around the age of 11-15 years when the child's facial growth is approaching completion. However, in some cases, early intervention may be necessary to correct functional or aesthetic issues, and rhinoplasty may be performed at an earlier age. 

Ultimately, the decision about when to perform rhinoplasty in cleft lip and palate patients is made on a case-by-case basis by the surgeon, in consultation with the patient and their family.

What is the best time to do palatoplasty?
three to six months old
nine to twelve months old
fifteen to twenty-four months old
twenty-four months and older

The best time to perform palatoplasty or cleft palate repair surgery is generally between nine to twelve months old. At this age, the anatomy of the palate is well-defined enough to allow for a successful repair, but early enough to prevent associated speech and feeding problems. 

However, the timing of palatoplasty may vary based on individual cases and the surgeon's preference. Some surgeons may perform the procedure slightly earlier or later depending on the infant's overall health, size, and other medical considerations.

In general, early repair of cleft palate is important to prevent speech difficulties and other issues with development of the oral cavity. In some cases, pre-surgical orthodontic treatment or nasoalveolar molding may be recommended prior to palatoplasty to further enhance the chances of a successful repair. 

Ultimately, the best timing for palatoplasty is determined on a case-by-case basis by the surgeon, in consultation with the patient's pediatrician or other medical professionals involved in their care.

Best time to be referred to a speech therapist?
nine to twelve months old
twelve to thirty-six months old
seven to ten years old
eleven to fifteen years old

The best time to be referred to a speech therapist can vary depending on the patient's specific needs and the underlying condition being treated. However, typically, the best time to be referred to a speech therapist is between twelve to thirty-six months old. 

This is the age range when children's speech and language skills typically begin to develop and when early intervention can make the most significant difference in their overall development. During this age range, a speech therapist can provide guidance and support for parents on how to promote language development in their child, as well as provide direct therapy services to address any underlying issues or delays.

However, it's important to note that speech therapy can be beneficial at any age, and referrals may also be made at other ages depending on the patient's needs and situation. Speech therapy can help individuals with a wide range of speech and language disorders, from infants with feeding and swallowing difficulties to older adults with speech impairments resulting from stroke or other neurological conditions.

Ultimately, referrals to a speech therapist should be made based on an individual assessment of the patient's needs and in consultation with the appropriate medical professionals.

Best age of first evaluation by an orthodontist.
zero to three months
three to six months
nine to twelve months
fifteen to eighteen months

choose

The American Association of Orthodontists generally recommends that a child have their first evaluation with an orthodontist by the age of seven years old. At this age, a child's orthodontic development and skeletal pattern can be evaluated to determine if there are any potential issues or developing problems in the teeth, jaws, or bite that may need correction.

The other age ranges mentioned (zero to three months, three to six months, nine to twelve months, and fifteen to eighteen months) are not commonly recommended as the first evaluation age by an orthodontist. While it is important for infants and young children to receive regular dental care and monitoring of their oral health, an evaluation by an orthodontist typically is not necessary until the child's permanent teeth have started to erupt, around the age of 6-7 years. 

However, if a child exhibits certain signs or symptoms such as difficulty biting, chewing, speaking, or breathing, they may be evaluated earlier by an orthodontist or other dental or medical professional. Additionally, children with certain risk factors such as craniofacial anomalies or a family history of dental or orthodontic issues may also require earlier evaluation and monitoring.
which of the choices is correct
I'm sorry, but I didn't see any choices to respond to your question. Can you please repost your question with the complete information so I can assist you better? Thank you!

Best age of first evaluation by an orthodontist.
zero to three months
three to six months
nine to twelve months
fifteen to eighteen months

which choice is correct

The correct response is: none of the above. 

The American Association of Orthodontists (AAO) recommends that children have their first evaluation with an orthodontist by age 7. At this age, enough permanent teeth have typically erupted to allow for evaluation of the child's orthodontic development and skeletal pattern. 

Infants and young children should still receive routine dental care beginning at age 1 to monitor the development and health of their teeth and mouth. However, routine orthodontic evaluations are generally not necessary until age 7 or later, unless there are concerns or specific issues that require earlier intervention or attention.

If surgical revisions are needed, when is the best time to do this?
1 to 3 years
3 to 7 years
7 to 10 years
11 to 15 years

The best time for surgical revisions following an initial cleft lip and/or palate repair is dependent upon the specific issues that require revision, as well as the patient's age and the timing of their previous surgery. 

In general, the best time for surgical revisions is after facial growth is mostly complete, which typically occurs around age 15 years or beyond. However, minor revisions may be performed earlier if necessary or if there is no significant risk to the patient's growth and development. 

It is important to ensure that the child's oral and facial development is being monitored by a multidisciplinary team and that recommendations for further surgical interventions are made based on a comprehensive examination and assessment of the child's individual needs.

A 4 years old complete unilateral cleft lip and palate patient was brought to you for consultation. What can you advise best to the parents?
the child does not need any surgical repair anymore
only cheiloplasty is needed
a combined cheiloplasty and palatoplasty has to be done ASAP
only palatoplasty is needed

For a 4-year-old patient with a complete unilateral cleft lip and palate, the best advice would be for a combined cheiloplasty and palatoplasty to be done as soon as possible. 

A complete unilateral cleft lip and palate requires a combination of surgical procedures to correct, including both a cheiloplasty to repair the cleft lip and a palatoplasty to repair the separated palate. Palatoplasty surgery typically needs to be done before the child is 18 months old to help avoid developmental problems with their speech and feeding. With the patient being 4 years old, it is important to move forward with a combined cheiloplasty and palatoplasty procedure as soon as possible to address any functional and aesthetic issues the child may be experiencing. 

It is important for the parents to seek guidance from a multidisciplinary team of healthcare professionals experienced in working with patients with cleft lip and palate, including a pediatrician, plastic surgeon, and speech therapist, among others. They can provide the parents with detailed information about the procedures and what to expect before, during, and after surgery, as well as provide ongoing support and care for the child's ongoing development.

A 22 year old male with a complete bilateral cleft lip and palate came to you for management of his defects. What can you advise best to this patient?
a combined cheiloplasty and palatoplasty has to be done ASAP
speech defect can easily be treated with doing both lip and palate repair
palate repair is very important and is needed to be done first
surgical repair is not a reassurance that speech defect can immediately improve

For a 22-year-old patient with a complete bilateral cleft lip and palate, the best advice would be that a combined cheiloplasty and palatoplasty may still be an option depending on his specific case. It is important to consult with a multidisciplinary team of healthcare professionals experienced in treating adult patients with cleft lip and palate to determine the most appropriate management plan for the individual.

In most cases, palatoplasty is very important and may need to be done before other procedures to enable proper speech, swallowing, and breathing function. For those with a history of previous repair, secondary or revision palatoplasty procedures may be considered to help improve speech and other functions.

While surgical repair is not a guarantee that speech defects will immediately improve, it can help to address functional issues and improve patient outcomes. Close follow-up with a speech therapist and
